coredata.dc:title: Preparation of EuVO4/graphene quantum dot composites and their gas-sensitive properties
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiang-Bing
Wang, An-Qi
Zhang, Qian-Qian
Zhou, Hui
Wu, Bao-Xu
Sha, Yun
Dang, Wen-Qiang
Ling, Wei-Jun
Dong, Zhong
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of nanocomposites comprising EuVO4/graphene quantum dot (GQDs) was achieved through a hydrothermal technique, employing precise temperature and duration parameters (180℃, 10 h), and the synthesis of EuVO4 was carried out via a hydrothermal approach, employing Eu(NO3)3·6H2O as the starting material. The nanocomposites underwent characterization using SEM, EDS, HRTEM, XRD, RAMAN, XPS and UV–Vis techniques. The results of the experiment indicate that the triethylamine vapor exhibits a favorable response and selectivity when detected by the sensor comprising EuVO4/GQDs nanocomposites. At 290℃, the sensor based on EuVO4/GQDs nanocomposites has a response of 21.536 to 100 ppm triethylamine, which is three times that of the intrinsic material. The time taken for 100 ppm triethylamine to respond and recover is 5 s and 10 s respectively, showing excellent cycle repeatability and stability, and has certain moisture resistance, it is possible to achieve a detection threshold as low as 1 ppm. Therefore, EuVO4/GQDs nanocomposite sensors are very promising for the rapid and efficient detection of triethylamine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: EuVO4
GQDs
Heterojunction
Gas sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of pure samples and composite materials
Preparation of europium vanadate by hydrothermal method
Preparation of europium vanadate composite graphene by hydrothermal method

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Education technology innovation project of Gansu Province (Grant No. 2024A-114), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 62165012), the Gansu Province College Industry Support Plan Projectand (Grant No.2024CYZC-44), the Gansu Provincial Department of Education: Major Cultivation Project of University Research and Innovation Platform (Grant Nos. 2024CXPT-12), the Youth Innovation and Entrepreneurship Talent Project of Gansu Province (Grant Number 2023LQGR22), the Young doctoral support program for universities of gansu Provincial (Grant No. 2023QB-005), the 2023 Key Talent Project of Gansu Province (Grant No.2023RCXM29), the Science and technology major special project of Gansu Province (Grant No. 22ZD6GE016, 23ZDGE001), the curriculum ideological and political project and teaching research project of Tianshui Normal University (Grant No. SFXM2021018, Grant No. SFKC20031, Grant No. JY202019, Grant No. PX-22184).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sensor preparation process and test process.
(a)XRD diffraction pattern; (b) EDS profile of EuVO4 and (c) EuVO4/GQDs.
XPS spectra of EuVO4/GQDs:(a) XPS survey; (b) C 1s peaks; (c) Eu 3d peaks; (d) V 2p peaks; (e) O 1s spectrum of EuVO4 and (f) O 1s peaks of EuVO4/GQDs.
SEM images of prepared samples: (a) EuVO4; (b) EuVO4/GQDs. (c)-(e) The element mapping of EuVO4 and (f)-(i) EuVO4/GQDs.
(a) The UV absorption spectra of EuVO4 and EuVO4/GQDs; (b) The optical band-gap fitting curve of EuVO4 and EuVO4/GQDs.
(a) The Raman spectrum of graphene quantum dots; (b) EuVO4 and (c) EuVO4/GQDs.

(a) Response curves of EuVO4 and EuVO4/GQDs sensors to different temperatures; (b) Selectivity test of EuVO4 and EuVO4/GQDs; (c) Repeatable test responses of EuVO4 and EuVO4/GQDs; (d) Response of EuVO4 and EuVO4/GQDs to different concentrations.
(a) The single adsorption desorption curve of EuVO4; (b) EuVO4/GQDs; (c) Response of EuVO4 / GQDs to 100 ppm triethylamine at low temperature and (d) Long term stability.
Response of EuVO4/GQDs composites to different humidity.
Gas-sensitive mechanism of EuVO4/GQDs in air and TEA.

head.ce:title: Preparation of EuVO4/graphene quantum dot composites and their gas-sensitive properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Integrated Circuit Packaging and Testing, Ministry of Education, Department of Physics, Tianshui Normal University, Tianshui, Gansu 741001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Integrated Circuit Packaging and Testing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center of Integrated Circuit Packaging and Testing, Ministry of Education, Department of Physics, Tianshui Normal University, Tianshui, Gansu 741001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of nanocomposites comprising EuVO4/graphene quantum dot (GQDs) was achieved through a hydrothermal technique, employing precise temperature and duration parameters (180℃, 10 h), and the synthesis of EuVO4 was carried out via a hydrothermal approach, employing Eu(NO3)3·6H2O as the starting material. The nanocomposites underwent characterization using SEM, EDS, HRTEM, XRD, RAMAN, XPS and UV–Vis techniques. The results of the experiment indicate that the triethylamine vapor exhibits a favorable response and selectivity when detected by the sensor comprising EuVO4/GQDs nanocomposites. At 290℃, the sensor based on EuVO4/GQDs nanocomposites has a response of 21.536 to 100 ppm triethylamine, which is three times that of the intrinsic material. The time taken for 100 ppm triethylamine to respond and recover is 5 s and 10 s respectively, showing excellent cycle repeatability and stability, and has certain moisture resistance, it is possible to achieve a detection threshold as low as 1 ppm. Therefore, EuVO4/GQDs nanocomposite sensors are very promising for the rapid and efficient detection of triethylamine.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: A gas detection device is designed to detect and analyze the concentrations of various gases present in close proximity [1], which holds significance in the monitoring of the environment, production within industries, healthcare, and various other domains [2]. The waste gas emitted by industrial activities not only affects the natural environment around human beings, but also seriously threatens human health [3]. Therefore, it is imperative to create a gas detection apparatus capable of efficiently and continuously monitoring the levels of hazardous gases that pose risks to both the environment and human body [4].

Triethylamine is an important organic compound, as a colorless volatile oily liquid, it has a strong ammonia taste [5]. Although triethylamine has a wide range of industrial applications, there are some safety hazards in its preparation and use [6]. Triethylamine possesses flammability properties and has the potential to create an explosive combination when its vapors come into contact with air[7]. Triethylamine exhibits significant respiratory tract irritation and has the potential to induce pulmonary edema and fatality upon inhalation [8]. Oral administration will corrode the mouth, esophagus and stomach, eyes and skin contact can cause chemical burns [9]. Real-time and precise detection of triethylamine is crucial in both daily life and industrial production, as it helps prevent any potential harm to human health. Nevertheless, there is a limited amount of research conducted on the advancement of high accuracy sensor designed specifically for trimethylamine [10,11].

Rare Earth Vanadates [12] are a class of compounds in which rare earth elements [13] (such as lanthanum, cerium [14], praseodymium, neodymium, samarium, europium [15], gadolinium, terbium, dysprosium, holmium, erbium [16], thulium, ytterbium, and lutetium) combine with vanadium (V) to form compounds with specific crystal structures and properties [17]. These compounds have a wide range of applications in optics, electronics, magnetism, catalysis and solid-state chemistry [18]. Rare earth vanadate also has high thermal stability, chemical stability, and has good gas sensitivity. These characteristics make it widely used in optical materials, laser materials, magneto-optical materials and various other disciplines [19]. Vanadates of rare earth elements exhibit magnetic properties, which is determined by their unique d electron and f electron structure, considerable atomic-level magnetic moment and notable impact of spin–orbit interaction. Some vanadates containing rare earth elements exhibit significant potential for utilization in the realm of photocatalysis, representing a novel category of exceptionally efficient photocatalysts. For example, bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) is a non-TiO2-based [20] visible light semiconductor photocatalyst [21] with ferroelectric and thermochromic properties [22]. In addition to the above properties, rare earth vanadate may also show excellent properties in dielectric materials [23], adsorption materials, catalysts [24] and chemical sensing. Semiconductor sensors are widely recognized for their exceptional ability to detect harmful gases and volatile organic compounds (VOC) owing to their cost-effectiveness, compact size, and high degree of control [25]. As a semiconductor with a wide band gap, EuVO4 exhibits commendable stability in its physicochemical characteristics. Consequently, it holds promising potential for utilization in various domains such as gas sensing, photocatalytic reactions, electrocatalysis, and other related applications.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) refer to a group of graphite nanomaterials, consisting of single, double, or multiple layers (ranging from 3 to 10), with a lateral dimension measuring approximately 100 nm [26]. GQDs possess exceptional attributes similar to graphene, such as a significant specific surface area, efficient surface grafting via p-p conjugate networks or surface functionalities, and diverse unique physical properties [26,27]; The high water solubility of GQDs can be attributed to the existence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on its outer edge, making it highly suitable for the continuous functionalization of inorganic materials [28] (see Fig. 1 ).

The process of manufacturing the gas sensor and conducting sample measurements is illustrated in Fig. 2 . As depicted in Fig. 2(a), a section of the sample was acquired and ground using a mortar. Subsequently, a small quantity of distilled water was added to form a paste, which was then uniformly applied onto the surface of the ceramic tube using a brush. The paste underwent maturation at 120℃ for 48 h prior to being evaluated with the WS-60B gas-sensitive measuring system.

The test principle is depicted in Fig. 2(b), showing Vh as the applied test voltage at 5 V and Vc as the heating voltage. Adjustments were made to the heating voltage in order to regulate the sensor's operating temperature during experimentation. Fig. 2(a) presents a cross-sectional perspective of a ceramic tube coated with a sample and equipped with four Pt wires. It is accompanied by a Ni-Cr alloy heating wire responsible for regulating the heating voltage. The comprehensive procedure for testing involves initiating the testing system, selecting an appropriate operating temperature, choosing a suitable load card to calibrate the baseline, and introducing the target gas into the test chamber through an injection hole. In the case of formaldehyde, the corresponding volume of formaldehyde liquid(concentration:99.5 %) was injected into the evaporation table in the test chamber through the injection hole, and the formaldehyde vapor was obtained by heating the evaporation table. This step was repeated by other gases. The sensor's gas response, denoted as S, is determined by either Rair/Rgas (for type n) or Rgas/Rair (for type p). The gas response and recovery time refers to the duration needed for the sensor resistance to reach 90 % saturation after exposure to or removal of gas.

Europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O), nitric acid (HNO3), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), HTMA hexamethyltetramine (C2H12N4), graphene quantum dots (100 nm), formaldehyde (CH2O), methanol (CH3OH), ethanol (C2H5OH), acetone (C3H6O), acetic acid (CH3COOH), triethylamine (C6H15N), benzene (C6H6) and ethanolamine (C2H7NO), xylene (C8H10), ethylene glycol (C2H6O2), n-butanol, isobutanol (C4H10O).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pure samples and composite materials
Preparation of europium vanadate by hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: Put 1.8 g of Eu (NO3)3·6H2O into the beaker, then add the appropriate amount of deionized water, add 9 ml of nitric acid to the solution under continuous stirring conditions. After nitric acid is added, 0.7 g of NH4VO3 is weighed, and the appropriate amount of ethylenediamine is added to adjust the PH value, and 1.4 g of HTMA is added at the end. The resulting solution was stirred continuously for 2 h. The resulting solution was moved to 100 ml telon coated stainless steel high pressure and placed in an oven (T = 180℃, reaction for 10 h). The sample A was subjected to alternate rinsing with deionized water and alcohol, followed by precipitation. The resulting precipitates were then transferred to a vacuum drying chamber set at 60 degrees for 24 h until a dry powder was obtained. Subsequently, the dry powder underwent annealing at a temperature of 700℃ for a duration of 2 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of europium vanadate composite graphene by hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: Add 0.0025 g GQDs to 27.5 ml of ethanol, sonicated stirring for 4 h, add the above solution to 0.25 g EuVO4 for 1 h, subject the mixture to autoclaving at a temperature of 180℃ for a duration of 24 h, followed by cooling to ambient temperature. Subsequently, perform alternating rinses with deionized water and ethanol, concluding with drying at 60 degrees for another 24 h.

The phase structure of the prepared materials was analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu-Kα radiation on a Bruker D8AA25X instrument. The microstructure of the samples was examined through scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) at an accelerated voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha instrument, utilizing Al Kα excitation radiation at 1486.6 eV. Surface charge correction was performed by referencing the standard C1s peak energy value combined with an energy of 284.8 eV. The analysis chamber had a sample analysis area diameter of 800 μm. Full spectrum scans were carried out with a passing energy of 100 eV and step size of 1 eV, while narrow-spectrum scanning employed a cut-off energy and step size of 50 and 0.05 eV, respectively. Raman spectra were acquired using a high-resolution confocal Raman microscope (Lab RAM HR Evolution from HORIBA), employing a 532 nm excitation source. UV–VIS absorbance measurements in the range of 200-800 nm were performed using a HITACHI U-3900H photometer.

The crystal structures of both pure EuVO4 and a composite material comprising EuVO4 and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were analyzed using the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique. This is illustrated in Fig. 3 (a), 2 theta is equal to 24.578°, 33.114°, 48.985°, 18.551°, 35.036°, 50.373°, 39.929°, 61.642°, 63.832°, 56.862°, 47.462°, 44.438°, 30.982°, 69.493°, and 72.913 °peaks corresponding to (200), (112), (312), (101), (220), (400), (301), (332), (204), (420), (321), (103), (211), (224) and (512) of EuVO4 [29]. It is a suitable match for the EuVO4 nanorods' pure tetragonal structure (BCC) with space group I41/amd (JCPDS card number 15-0809) [30]. 2 theta is equal to 24.586°, 33.134°, 48.999°, 18.555°, 35.047°, 50.405°, 61.565° and 39.94 2° corresponding to EuVO4/GQDs (200), (112), (312), (101), (220), (400), (332) and (301) peaks. The presence of well-defined and distinct diffraction peaks suggests that the crystallization level of the EuVO4/GQDs composites is satisfactory. There were no detectable impurity peaks observed, indicating a high level of purity in the composition of EuVO4/GQDs. With the introduction of GQDs, the intensity of diffraction peaks increases significantly, indicating the interaction between EuVO4 and GQDs. Furthermore, it has been observed that the X-ray diffraction pattern does not exhibit the presence of a diffraction peak for GQDs. This absence can be attributed to the fact that the dopant concentration in GQDs is below the detection limit of the X-ray diffractometer. It also shows that a small amount of GQDs doping does not cause the change of EuVO4 crystal type. Fig. 3(b) and 3(c) the EDS spectra provide information on the composition and relative abundance of Eu, V, C, and O elements in both EuVO4 and composites of EuVO4/GQDs.

The purpose of the XPS analysis was to examine the elemental compositions and valence states exhibited by composites of EuVO4/GQDs. As depicted in Fig. 4 (a), the comprehensive XPS spectrum of EuVO4/GQDs exhibited the existence of elements including Eu, V, O, and C. The peak observed at C1s can be attributed to carbon present in GQDs. Additionally, it is worth noting that there might have been some external contamination during the XPS measurement process. To ensure accuracy, a peak correction value of 284.8 eV for C1s was applied to other elements.

In the case of Fig. 4(c), the XPS spectra display distinct peaks at 1134.52 eV and 1164.11 eV, representing the energy levels of trivalent Eu3d3/2 and 3d5/2 states respectively. Additionally, there are two distinct satellite peaks adjacent to Eu3d5/2 and 3d3/2 levels. Moving on to Fig. 4(d), the splitting signal observed in V2p is attributed to the maximum points of binding energy at 525.29 eV (V2p1/2) and 517.62 eV (V2p3/2), which can be associated with V5+ [5]. There are two distinct peaks observed at 516.98 eV and 524.28 eV, suggesting the existence of a minor quantity of V4 + within the analyzed sample.

The spectrum of O1s at a high resolution is depicted in Fig. 4(f), which exhibits three distinct components upon decomposition. The observed peak at 530.80 eV can be attributed to the existence of oxygen vacancies (OV). The crystal formation occurs due to atomic rearrangement during the annealing procedure in the calcination furnace. However, certain oxygen atoms with less stable bonds may displace from their original positions and create vacancies. The increase in the number of oxygen vacancies can provide additional sites for desired molecules to be adsorbed. Therefore, the composite material comprising EuVO4/GQDs effectively enhances the gas sensing capabilities by promoting the interaction between oxygen and gas molecules. The presence of lattice oxygen (OL) is responsible for the occurrence of the second peak observed at 529.85 eV, whereas chemisorbed oxygen (OC) can be attributed to the detection of the third peak at 532.13 eV. It is worth mentioning that when compared to the EuVO4 depicted in Fig. 4(e), the significant enhancement observed in EuVO4/GQDs can be attributed to the presence of oxygen vacancies and chemisorbed oxygen. This leads to an augmented number of accessible active sites for the surface adsorption and diffusion of oxygen molecules, thereby enhancing the sensing capabilities of the material.

The SEM image of EuVO4 material in Fig. 5 (a) reveals a tetragonal structure with blocky morphology, exhibiting a relatively smooth exposed surface. In contrast, the SEM image in Fig. 5(b) illustrates the rough and uneven block structure of the composite material formed by incorporating GQDs into EuVO4. Compared to the morphology of EuVO4, it is evident that the surface of EuVO4/GQDs exhibits attachment of GQDs nanosheets. Considering that the size of the GQDs used is relatively large, the SEM analysis can observe the nanosheets composed of GQDs. GQDs nanosheets endow EuVO4 with surface roughness while maintaining its basic morphology. GQDs nanosheets introduce surface roughness to EuVO4, while maintaining its basic morphology. Despite the uneven surface, the regular block structure remains visible. The enhanced gas sensitivity of the composite can be attributed to the increased surface roughness, resulting in a higher specific surface area and more available sites for adsorption. This promotes efficient reactions between gas molecules and reactive oxygen species on the sensitive EuVO4/GQDs surface, facilitating gas adsorption. It is evident from the image that there exists a seamless connection between EuVO4 and GQDs, indicating the presence of interface interaction. Consequently, the heightened gas sensitivity can potentially be attributed to the development of a heterogeneous architecture between EuVO4 and GQDs.

Fig. 5(c)-(e) shows the Mapping of EuVO4 materials, with Eu, V and O elements uniformly distributed, which proves that the prepared EuVO4 is high purity. Fig. 5(f)-(i) shows the Mapping of EuVO4/GQDs materials. It is evident that the distribution of the C element occurs on the surface of EuVO4, but it is not uniform. Considering that GQDs has a sheet structure, it provides a high surface area for the material, which improves the gas sensitive performance.

To gain deeper insights into the morphology and microstructure of composites consisting of EuVO4 and EuVO4/GQDs, we conducted TEM analysis. Fig. 6 (a)-(b) displays a TEM image of composites consisting of EuVO4/GQDs, providing additional evidence that the structure of EuVO4/GQDs is predominantly tetragonal with graphene nanosheets adhering to the surface of EuVO4. Fig. 6(c) displays an HRTEM image of the composite formed by EuVO4/GQDs, the distinct stripe spacings observed were found to be 0.36 nm and 0.214 nm, corresponding precisely to the crystal surface indexed as (200). Additionally, a lattice stripe with a length of 0.208 nm is observed, corresponding to the crystal surface (103). In Fig. 6(d), the SAED pattern of the EuVO4/GQDs composites demonstrates diffraction rings that further confirmed that it is a mixture of single crystal and polycrystalline.

Fig. 7 (a) displays the absorption spectra of EuVO4 and EuVO4/GQDs in the UV–VIS range, showcasing distinct absorption edges. The absorption edge for EuVO4 is observed within the range of 550–600 nm, while for EuVO4/GQDs it occurs at approximately 350–400 nm. Compared to EuVO4, the absorption range of the composite material EuVO4/GQDs is shifted towards the ultraviolet region within the 250 nm-350 nm band. Moreover, there is an increase in absorption intensity, indicating that coupling EuVO4 with GQDs enhances its capacity for absorbing ultraviolet light. Additionally, the absorption intensity of EuVO4/GQDs at wavelengths between 500 nm and 800 nm also exhibits a significant improvement compared to that of pure EuVO4.

The band gap width can be used by Tauc formula:
(1) aℎv = A(ℎv − Eg)n

By evaluating the correlation between (αℎv)2 and photon energy (ℎv), it can be inferred that the presence of EuVO4/GQDs heterojunction leads to a noticeable alteration in the band gap width compared to pure EuVO4. The bandgap width of EuVO4 and EuVO4/GQDs is demonstrated in Fig. 7(b), indicating that the composite can effectively reduce the material's band gap with proper treatment. It can be inferred that the incorporation of GQDs into EuVO4 results in a reduction in the width of the energy band gap of the EuVO4/GQDs composite, consequently lowering the energy needed for electron transitions between the conduction and valence bands.

Fig. 8 illustrates the Raman spectra of graphene quantum dots, EuVO4, and EuVO4/GQDs. The D and G peaks of GQDs can be identified by observing the two distinctive peaks at 1340 and 1588 cm−1 in Fig. 8(c). The G-band is typically produced by the in-plane oscillation of sp2 carbon atoms, providing a reliable indication of the quantity of graphene layers [31]. The D-band arises from the departure of lattice vibrations from the central region of Leen, serving as a means to assess imperfections in the structure of graphene [27]. The strength of the G and D bands increases with an increase in the number of graphene layers, as it leads to a higher presence of defects [32].

In the experiment depicted in Fig. 9 (a), we assessed the performance of a gas sensor manufactured within a temperature range of 250–350 °C. The evaluation involved measuring its sensitivity to triethylamine at a concentration of 100 ppm. The response pattern exhibited by the sensor resembled a distinctive curve, similar to that of a mountain shape. At reduced temperatures, the sensing response exhibited a relatively limited strength as the triethylamine gas molecules lacked adequate thermal energy to surpass the activation energy threshold necessary for initiating the reaction. However, as the temperature increased, the triethylamine molecule acquired enough energy to trigger an elevation in the sensor's response. All sensors were observed showing higher sensitivity to triethylamine at a temperature of 290℃. Further increases in temperature resulted in a decrease in the sensing response, likely due to increased desorption of triethylamine molecules before participation in the reaction. The inset in Fig. 9(a) shows the change of Ra with temperature. First, as the temperature rises, the atoms begin to vibrate violently, causing the collision of the free electron and other electrons. This collision results in the energy loss of the free electrons, which form the electric current. Due to the energy consumption, the electron drift velocity decreases, resulting in an increased resistance of the material. The temperature continues to rise, and the ionized carriers increase, counteracting the lattice vibration of the atoms, so the resistance drops. Because Ra was maximum at 290℃ and the response was highest, at this time, an operating temperature of 290℃ was selected for all subsequent measurements.

The selectivity of the gas sensor refers to its ability to differentiate the target gas from other gases in a mixed gas environment, serving as a critical performance indicator that determines the level of interference resistance exhibited by the gas sensor. To evaluate the selectivity, experiments were conducted on EuVO4 and EuVO4/GQDs gas sensors placed in different atmospheres at their optimal operating temperatures, as shown in Fig. 9(b). The target gases in this study include formaldehyde (CH2O), methanol (CH3OH), ethanol (C2H5OH), acetone (C3H6O), acetic acid (CH3COOH), triethylamine (C6H15N), benzene (C6H6) and ethanolamine (C2H7NO), xylene (C8H10), ethylene glycol (C2H6O2), n-butanol, and isobutanol(C4H10O). The findings indicate that EuVO4 exhibited the highest response to 100 ppm triethylamine with a response value of 6.9. In contrast, when combined with GQDs, EuVO4/GQDs demonstrated an even stronger response to 100 ppm triethylamine, reaching as high as 20.8. This represents approximately three times greater sensitivity compared to intrinsic EuVO4. This is a result of incorporating a specific quantity of GQDs into EuVO4 to minimize the band gap in EuVO4/GQDs composites. The reduction in the energy required for electron transition from the conduction band to the valence band leads to an enhanced reaction. However, there was no significant improvement in the response to gases other than triethylamine gas. In fact, the response to formaldehyde, benzene, and ethanolamine gas also decreased. These findings indicate that the TEA exhibits the highest selectivity among all gases tested for the EuVO4/GQDs sensor.

Repeatability plays a crucial role in the functionality of devices. Fig. 9(c) displays the response-recovery curves of EuVO4 and EuVO4/GQDs sensors after exposure to 100 ppm triethylamine for six cycles, demonstrating favorable reversibility in response recovery and reproducibility across multiple cycles for both sensors.

The effects of EuVO4 and EuVO4/GQDs sensors were evaluated at various concentrations (1 ppm, 5 ppm, 10 ppm, 15 ppm, 25 ppm, 50 ppm, 75 ppm, 100 ppm, and 150 ppm) under the optimized operating temperature of 290℃. Additionally, response values were measured for higher concentrations (200 ppm, 500 ppm, and 1000 ppm). The response values of EuVO4 are 1.2028, 1.5279, 1.6752, 1.9491, 2.8818, 3.6624, 5.1915, 6.4632, 8.2604, 8.7471, 10.4617, and 12.2722; The response values of EuVO4/GQDs are 1.5304, 2.2247, 3.3320, 3.4798, 4.8586, 7.5275, 12.6172, 13.7799, 18.9938, 25.1513, 52.3379 and 70.9420. The graph depicts a linear regression curve, indicating a strong and significant linear correlation between the sensor response value and gas concentration. The EuVO4/GQDs sensor exhibits exceptional selectivity and sensitivity towards triethylamine gas, as indicated by its remarkably low detection limit of 1 ppm. Compared to the EuVO4 sensor, the inclusion of GQDs in EuVO4 enhances its response value, suggesting that incorporating a specific quantity of GQDs into EuVO4 can enhance both its selectivity and high sensitivity.

Fig. 10 (a)-(b) illustrates the reaction − restoration curve of the two sensors when exposed to 100 ppm triethylamine gas at their respective optimal operating temperatures. It is evident that both the EuVO4 sensor and the EuVO4/GQDs sensor exhibit a response time and recovery time of 10 s. However, in the case of the EuVO4/GQDs sensor, there is a reduction in adsorption time to 5 s. Meanwhile, there was a significant increase in the reaction. The rapid response time of the sensor plays a crucial role in safeguarding human life and property. This can be attributed to the extensive surface area of the EuVO4/GQDs heterostructure, which enhances gas adsorption, as well as the efficient migration of carriers within this heterostructure. Fig. 10(c) illustrates the response values of EuVO4/GQDs towards 100 ppm triethylamine at a lower temperature. At 20℃, the response of EuVO4/GQDs was recorded as 1.1086, while at temperatures ranging from 60 to 100℃, the responses were measured as 1.2024, 1.3008, 1.4125, 1.7165 and 1.9653 respectively. The enduring stability of the sensor is demonstrated in Fig. 10(d), as indicated by its consistent response over an extended period of time with minimal fluctuations.

Based on research findings, it is crucial to evaluate the sensor's response under different humidity levels due to the competition between target gas and water molecules for adsorbed oxygen. Fig. 11 illustrates the sensor's response at various humidity levels (ranging from 5 %RH to 60 %RH) when exposed to 100 ppm TEA while maintaining an operating temperature of 290℃. The EuVO4/GQDs-based gas sensor exhibits minimal variation in response between 5 %RH and 45 %RH, with an average response value of approximately 7.8. This suggests that most active sites on the surface of EuVO4/GQDs sensing materials are predominantly occupied by TEA molecules, while water molecules occupy these sites to a lesser extent. Consequently, within the range of humidity levels from 5 % to 45 %RH, the impact of water molecules on the TEA adsorption process can be considered negligible. However, once the relative humidity surpasses 45 %RH, there is a noticeable decrease in response values: specifically, 3.1518 (50 %RH), 1.9053 (55 %RH), and 1.7457 (60 %RH). This decline can be attributed to the occupation of numerous gas adsorption active sites by water molecules. Reactive OH− gradually forms a chemisorbed layer on the surface of EuVO4/GQDs, which obstructs the adsorption of oxygen anions and hampers the diffusion pathway for TEA molecules. Consequently, it restricts the interaction between the target gas and the sensing material, leading to a decline in response value. Despite being negatively impacted by high humidity conditions (60 %RH), the EuVO4/GQDs gas sensor still exhibits a significant response of 1.7457. The humidity sensitivity test confirms that the gas sensor based on EuVO4/GQDs composite material possesses certain resistance against interference caused by humidity.

Many scholars have conducted research on the mechanism behind the formation of graphene-metal oxide [33]. There is a higher concentration of oxygen-containing groups located at the periphery of GQDs compared to graphene [34]. The possible mechanism behind the formation of the EuVO4/GQDs complex could be attributed to the electron-rich nature of GQDs, which possess a significant number of oxygen-containing groups such as hydroxyl, epoxy, and carbonyl groups located at their edges. These functional groups on GQDs create an ideal platform for facilitating electrostatic interactions between the positively charged EuVO4 and negatively charged oxide functional group present on GQDs. The electrostatic force facilitates the attachment of EuVO4 ions to GQDs. When HTMA is present, the surface-bound Eu3 + ions on GQDs undergo a hydrothermal process to generate nanocrystals of EuVO4.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) exhibit p-type semiconductor characteristics, while EuVO4 acts as an n-type metal oxide semiconductor. The combination of these two substances leads to the formation of a heterojunction at their interface. The establishment of equilibrium in the Fermi level induces a modification in the band structure through electron transfer from EuVO4 to GQDs and hole movement from GQDs to EuVO4. By effectively illustrating this process, the charge density difference (CDD) provides valuable insights into electron properties. Numerous studies have reported on how varying the ratio between graphene and metal oxides influences the gas-sensitive characteristics of metal/graphene composites. The improved response could be attributed to the formation of a heterojunction between GQDs and EuVO4, leading to enhanced internal electronic conductivity within the composite. The incorporation of GQDs-EuVO4 matrix significantly enhances the composite's ability to conduct electricity internally. Furthermore, the incorporation of surface imperfections in GQDs and the presence of oxygen-containing groups serve as favorable sites for gas molecules, thereby augmenting the response of EuVO4/GQDs composites.

When the surface of EuVO4/GQDs nanosheets comes into contact with triethylamine gas molecules, there are two primary explanations for the mechanism by which the gas sensitivity occurs. Firstly, it involves the adsorption reaction of oxygen species associated with EuVO4, as depicted in Fig. 12 . Upon exposure to air, EuVO4 interacts with oxygen molecules present in the atmosphere and accepts electrons from its conduction band, resulting in the formation of O- and O2–. This process leads to a reduction in carrier concentration and an increase in resistance.

Therefore, the EuVO4/GQDs sensor demonstrates superior performance compared to other chemisorbed oxygen ion sensors when operated at the optimal temperature of 290℃. Triethylamine acts as a reducing gas and upon exposure to gas-sensitive materials, it undergoes a reaction with oxygen ions (O-) resulting in the formation of CO2, NO2, and H2O. The reaction is as follows:
(2) O2 (gas) + 2e- → 2O− (ads) (100 ◦C < T < 300 ◦C)
(3) C6H15N + O− + e- → CO2 + H2O + NO2 (100 ◦C < T < 300 ◦C)

In the course of the reaction, the composite material experiences a reduction in hole density due to the liberation of electrons and holes on its surface. This subsequently leads to an increase in material resistance. Consequently, there is a release of electrons from the adsorbed oxygen substance and alteration in grain boundary barrier, resulting in modifications to gas sensor conductivity and subsequent changes in material resistance [35]. On the other hand, with the addition of a small quantity of GQDs in the composite, there was a significant reduction in the self-resistance of the composite materials. GQDs possess a quasi-zero-dimensional layered structure that offers an extensive specific surface area, excellent electrical conductivity, and rapid carrier mobility. These properties facilitate an increased contact area between the material and the target gas, thereby enhancing its gas sensitivity. The presence of GQDs in EuVO4 enhances the surface diffusion reaction of triethylamine, leading to an increased number of active sites and enhanced adsorption capacity for triethylamine. Consequently, EuVO4/GQDs exhibits superior gas-sensitive properties compared to intrinsic EuVO4. Moreover, the material's high crystalline quality hinders the combination or separation of adsorbed oxygen species on its surface, thereby preventing a decrease in sensor response value. As a result, EuVO4/GQDs demonstrates exceptional sensitivity towards triethylamine.

The presence of GQDs significantly impacts the gas-sensitive response and selectivity of EuVO4/GQDs nanocomposites. Experimental findings demonstrate that the sensor utilizing EuVO4/GQDs nanocomposites exhibits excellent sensitivity and specificity towards triethylamine vapor, surpassing other vapors by more than threefold in terms of response value. The EuVO4/GQDs nanocomposite sensor, which was prepared in this study, exhibits a response value of 21.536 towards triethylamine at a concentration of 100 ppm and a temperature of 290℃. Additionally, it shows a response value of 1.5304 even when the gas concentration is as low as 1 ppm. The response time for detecting triethylamine at a concentration of 100 ppm is only 5 s, while the recovery time is just 10 s. These results demonstrate that our developed sensor has faster and more stable performance compared to the inherent adsorption capability of EuVO4 alone. In summary, the combination of EuVO4 and GQDs exhibits promising potential as a sensing material for effectively detecting trace amounts of triethylamine.

Xiang-Bing Li: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. An-Qi Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Qian-Qian Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Hui Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Bao-Xu Wu: Writing – review & editing. Yun Sha: Visualization. Wen-Qiang Dang: Funding acquisition. Wei-Jun Ling: Funding acquisition. Zhong Dong: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Education technology innovation project of Gansu Province (Grant No. 2024A-114), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 62165012), the Gansu Province College Industry Support Plan Projectand (Grant No.2024CYZC-44), the Gansu Provincial Department of Education: Major Cultivation Project of University Research and Innovation Platform (Grant Nos. 2024CXPT-12), the Youth Innovation and Entrepreneurship Talent Project of Gansu Province (Grant Number 2023LQGR22), the Young doctoral support program for universities of gansu Provincial (Grant No. 2023QB-005), the 2023 Key Talent Project of Gansu Province (Grant No.2023RCXM29), the Science and technology major special project of Gansu Province (Grant No. 22ZD6GE016, 23ZDGE001), the curriculum ideological and political project and teaching research project of Tianshui Normal University (Grant No. SFXM2021018, Grant No. SFKC20031, Grant No. JY202019, Grant No. PX-22184).
